era_context: The late Augustan age, with Augustus still living and in his 74th year. The Roman Empire is at peace and at the height of its territorial extent. In China, the Western Han dynasty has recently fallen (9 CE); Wang Mang's Xin dynasty is in its second year and beginning to face serious problems. In India, various kingdoms coexist; the Satavahana dynasty is rising. This is a year of relative stability in the Mediterranean and increasing crisis in China.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse written records)
Interior of the Americas (no written records)

events.title: Augustus consolidates power; Tiberius groomed for succession
events.description: Augustus, now in his 74th year, is in declining health but still actively governing. He is carefully grooming Tiberius for succession, ensuring that the military and Senate are aligned. Tiberius is given increasing authority and responsibility, preparing him for sole rule. The succession is expected to be smooth, though there is some uncertainty about whether Tiberius will be accepted by the Senate and people.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's age and health in 11 CE are well documented. The preparations for succession are attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Leads to 14-001 (Death of Augustus and accession of Tiberius)

events.title: Wang Mang's Xin dynasty faces economic crisis and bandit uprisings
events.description: Wang Mang's Xin dynasty, now in its second year, is facing severe economic problems. The emperor's radical land reforms and currency manipulations are proving unpopular and destabilizing. Bandit activity is increasing in the countryside as desperate peasants turn to robbery. The government is struggling to maintain control. The dynasty is entering a period of crisis that will lead to its collapse within a decade.

sources.name: Hanshu (History of the Han), compiled by Ban Gu
Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han), compiled by Fan Ye

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records document the crises of Wang Mang's reign in detail. The chronology of economic problems and bandit uprisings is well established.

events.title: Roman military presence on Rhine and Danube frontiers
events.region: Rhine and Danube frontiers, Germania and Pannonia
events.description: Roman legions are stationed on the Rhine and Danube frontiers, maintaining control of the empire's borders. The legions are well-trained and well-equipped, and they maintain order along the frontier. Relations with Germanic tribes are tense but not openly hostile. The frontier is stable, though there are periodic raids and skirmishes.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Rhine and Danube frontier forts

events.certainty_note: The Roman military presence on the Rhine and Danube frontiers is well attested in archaeological evidence and in Tacitus's account.
cross_references: Leads to 14-002 (Mutinies of Rhine and Danube legions)

events.title: Parthian Empire maintains control of eastern frontier
events.description: The Parthian Empire continues to control the eastern frontier and the Silk Road trade routes. Relations with Rome remain tense but not openly hostile. The Parthians are the major power in the Near East and serve as a buffer between Rome and the Indian Ocean trade networks.
events.certainty_note: The Parthian Empire's control of the eastern frontier is well attested. The chronology of individual rulers is uncertain.

events.title: Satavahana dynasty consolidates power in central India
events.description: The Satavahana dynasty continues to consolidate its power in the Deccan and central India. The dynasty is issuing coins and inscriptions that attest to its authority and territorial control. Trade routes through Satavahana territory are becoming increasingly important for commerce between the Mediterranean and the Indian Ocean.
events.certainty_note: The Satavahana dynasty is well attested in numismatic and epigraphic evidence, but the precise chronology is debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative emphasizes Augustus's careful and deliberate preparation for succession, suggesting a smooth and orderly transition. However, some modern scholars argue that the succession was less certain than it appeared and that there were significant political tensions beneath the surface. The economic crisis of Wang Mang's Xin dynasty is sometimes portrayed as a linear decline, but recent scholarship suggests that the situation was more complex, with periods of relative stability interspersed with crises.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 11 CE is rich for the Roman Empire and China. Roman sources are detailed but written 80–200 years after the events. Chinese sources are similarly detailed and reliable. For India and the broader Indian Ocean world, sources are sparse and primarily numismatic or archaeological. The Americas, sub-Saharan Africa, and northern Europe are absent from the written record.

graph_edges.note: Augustus's preparations for succession lead to Tiberius's accession in 14 CE
The economic crisis of Wang Mang's Xin dynasty in 11 CE leads to Yellow River flooding in 12 CE
The Roman military presence on the Rhine and Danube frontiers leads to mutinies in 14 CE
